sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=51, col=40).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 90, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 18 of about 33 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 17 actions returned the board to a state already seen 17 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 17 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the ascii, I can see:
- Row 8: BwBBBBBBBBBBBBBB - white pixel at col 19
- Row 9: BBwwBBBBBBBBBBBB - white pixels at cols 20-21
- Row 10: BBBBwOOOBBBBBBBB - white at col 23, orange at cols 24-26
- Row 11: BBBBOOOOOBBwwwww - orange at cols 22-26, white at cols 31-35
- Row 12: BBBBOOMOOwwBBBBB - orange at cols 22-23, 25-26, magenta at col 24, white at cols 29-30
- Row 13: BBBBOOOOOBBBBBBB - orange at cols 22-26
- Row 14: BBwwBOOOBBBBBBBB - white at cols 18-19, orange at cols 21-23
- Row 15: wwBBBBBBBBBBBBBB - white at cols 16-17
- Row 22: BBBBBBBSSSSSSSSS - water starts at col 21
- Row 23: BBBBBBSSSSSSSSSS - water starts at col 20
- Row 24: BBBSSSSSSSSSSSSS - water starts at col 17
- Row 25: BSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS - water starts at col 15
So the chain (white + orange) is at rows 8-15, and water starts at row 22. The chain is above the water.
Looking at the layout:
- White diamond center around (12, 24) based on the pattern
- Orange diamond center around (12, 24) - wait, the orange has magenta inside at (12, 24)
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Row 10: BBBBwOOOBBBBBBBB - the chain has a white pixel at col 23, then orange at 24-26
- Row 12: BBBBOOMOOwwBBBBB - orange at 22-23, magenta at 24, orange at 25-26, white at 29-30
- Row 13: BBBBOOOOOBBBBBBB - orange at 22-26
So the orange diamond is centered around (12, 24) with magenta inside.
The white pixels form a chain extending from the orange: going up-left to (8, 19) and down-left to (15, 16-17), and also extending to the right to (11, 31-35) and (12, 29-30).
The water is at rows 22-25, cols 15-38 (approximately).
To pull the chain down toward the orange ring, I need to click water below the chain. The chain's bottom is around row 15, and water starts at row 22.
I should click water at (22, 24) which is directly below the chain.
But wait, in the previous attempt, clicking water at (27, 26) pulled the chain down. The chain was at (12-16, 26-30) and water was at (22-32, 14-38). Clicking (27, 26) was within the water region.
Now the chain is at a different position. Let me click water at (22, 24) to pull the chain down.
Actually, looking at the ascii, row 22 col 24 would be:
Row 22: BBBBBBBSSSSSSSSS
         0123456789...
Col 21 is the first S. So col 24 is S. Good, that's water.
Let me click (22, 24).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Now I see the layout clearly. Chain at rows 8-15, water starts at row 22. Need to click water below chain to pull it down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click water at (22, 24) - directly below the chain
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 24}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== After clicking (22,24) ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'G', 'O', 'p', 'S', 'M'] and n['pixels'] > 5:
        rows = [b[0] for b in n['boundary']]
        cols = [b[1] for b in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
print("State:", last_action_result.get('state'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

S: pixels=138, rows=29-47, cols=52-63
